Check for ketones in the urine.

questions.rationale: Administering rapid-acting insulin would worsen hypoglycemia. Checking for ketones is relevant for hyperglycemia, not hypoglycemia. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Diabetes Management
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a client with type 2 diabetes has a blood glucose level of 45 mg/dl and is confused. which intervention should the nurse implement first?

choices.text: Give orange juice with added sugar

questions.rationale: For conscious clients with hypoglycemia, oral glucose is the first intervention. Orange juice with sugar provides fast-acting carbohydrates to quickly raise blood glucose levels. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Holistic Nursing Care
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a patient is experiencing significant anxiety related to a recent diagnosis. which nursing intervention demonstrates a holistic approach to care?

choices.text: Facilitating communication with family and support systems.

questions.rationale: A holistic approach involves addressing the patient's physical, emotional, and social needs. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Infection Control
questions.text: According to nursing standards, which of the following are standard precautions used to prevent the spread of infection? select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Standard precautions apply to all patients regardless of their diagnosis. Airborne and contact precautions are used in addition to standard precautions for specific infections. Sterile technique is not always required. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medication Administration
questions.text: According to nursing standards, before administering any medication, the nurse should:

choices.text: Ask the patient their name and date of birth.
Verify the medication order with the MAR.

questions.rationale: All three actions are essential steps in medication administration to ensure patient safety. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medication Calculation
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a patient is prescribed digoxin 0.125 mg daily. the medication is available in 0.25 mg tablets. how many tablets should the nurse administer?
questions.rationale: 0.125 mg / 0.25 mg/tablet = 0.5 tablets. The nurse would administer half a tablet. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medication Calculation
questions.text: According to nursing standards, the healthcare provider orders heparin 1,000 units/hour iv. the label reads 25,000 units/500 ml. calculate the ml/hour to be administered.
questions.rationale: Using dimensional analysis: (1,000 units/hour × 500 mL) ÷ 25,000 units = 20 mL/hour This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Postoperative Assessment
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a priority assessment for a postoperative patient is:
questions.rationale: All three are crucial for postoperative patient safety and recovery. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Postoperative Care
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a priority assessment for a postoperative patient is:
questions.rationale: All three are crucial for postoperative patient safety and recovery. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a nurse is assessing a client's vital signs. which parameter is considered the fifth vital sign?
questions.rationale: Pain is often referred to as the 'fifth vital sign' to emphasize its importance in patient assessment along with temperature, pulse, respiration, and blood pressure. Pain assessment is critical for comprehensive care and improved outcomes. Oxygen saturation (A), level of consciousness (C), and pupillary response (D) are important assessments but are not considered vital signs. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a nurse is caring for multiple patients. which nursing action requires hand hygiene before and after the procedure?
questions.rationale: Hand hygiene should be performed before and after ALL patient interactions and procedures, including documentation (A), administering medications (B), and taking vital signs (C). This is a fundamental infection control practice to prevent the spread of pathogens between patients and healthcare providers. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a nurse is preparing to transfer a patient from bed to a chair. which assessment is most important before initiating the transfer?

choices.text: The patient's ability to bear weight and follow instructions
The distance between the bed and chair

questions.rationale: Assessing the patient's ability to bear weight and follow instructions is critical for safety before attempting a transfer. This determines whether the patient can assist with the transfer and what type of assistance is needed. While vital signs (A), weight (B), and distance (D) are considerations, the patient's physical capability and cognitive status are the most important factors for a safe transfer. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: In a hospital setting, a nurse is preparing to administer medications to a client. to ensure client safety, which action should the nurse perform first?

choices.text: Perform the third medication check at the bedside.
Check the client's identification using two identifiers.
Explain the purpose of the medication to the client.

questions.rationale: Ensuring the 'Right Patient' is a crucial first step in the medication administration process according to the 'Rights of Medication Administration'. This is accomplished by using at least two client identifiers (e.g., name and date of birth, name and medical record number) and comparing them against the medication administration record (MAR). While explaining the medication (C) and performing checks (D) are vital, verifying the patient's identity (B) must occur before proceeding. Documentation (A) occurs after administration. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Fundamentals of Nursing
questions.text: In a hospital setting, a nurse witnesses another healthcare provider failing to follow proper hand hygiene protocols before caring for a client. what is the nurse's primary ethical responsibility in this situation?

choices.text: Report the incident directly to the hospital administrator.
Address the issue directly with the healthcare provider involved.
Document the observation in the client's medical record.
Ignore the observation as it was a single occurrence.

questions.rationale: The primary ethical responsibility is patient safety. The most direct and often most effective initial step is to address the concern directly and professionally with the colleague (C). This provides an opportunity for immediate correction and education. If the behavior persists or is egregious, escalating the concern through the chain of command (e.g., charge nurse, manager) is necessary. Reporting directly to administration (A) without attempting collegial correction is not the first step. Documenting in the patient record (B) is not appropriate for staff issues. Ignoring a breach in infection control practice (D) puts patients at risk and fails to uphold professional standards. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a client diagnosed with heart failure. which assessment findings would indicate worsening of the condition? (select all that apply.)

choices.text: Weight loss of 1 kg in 24 hours
Presence of crackles in the lung bases
Pitting edema rated at 3+ in lower extremities
Client reports increased shortness of breath when lying flat

questions.rationale: Worsening heart failure typically involves increased fluid volume overload. Crackles (B) indicate pulmonary edema (left-sided failure). Increased pitting edema (C) and JVD (F) indicate systemic venous congestion (right-sided failure). Orthopnea (shortness of breath when lying flat) (D) indicates increased pulmonary congestion. Weight gain (not loss, A) and increased abdominal girth (due to ascites, not E) would also signify worsening fluid retention. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a client with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (copd). which finding would indicate a potential need for oxygen therapy adjustment or evaluation?

choices.text: Oxygen saturation (SpO2) of 91% on room air at rest

questions.rationale: In some COPD clients, particularly those with chronic hypercapnia (CO2 retention), excessive oxygen administration can depress the hypoxic drive to breathe, leading to increased CO2 retention, respiratory acidosis, and CNS effects like confusion or drowsiness (carbon dioxide narcosis) (C). While SpO2 of 91% (B) might be acceptable or baseline for some COPD clients, a change in mental status is a more urgent sign requiring assessment. A respiratory rate of 18 (A) and minimal sputum production (D) are not concerning findings in this context. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing (Immune)
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client receiving a blood transfusion develops flushing, itching, and hives within the first 15 minutes. what is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Flushing, itching (pruritus), and hives (urticaria) are signs of a potential allergic reaction, which can range from mild to severe (anaphylaxis). The absolute first priority when any transfusion reaction is suspected is to stop the transfusion immediately (C) to prevent further administration of the offending agent. Subsequent actions include assessing vital signs (D), maintaining IV access with normal saline, notifying the provider and blood bank, and administering medications like antihistamines (A) as ordered. Slowing the rate (B) is never appropriate when a reaction is suspected. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing (Musculoskeletal)
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client has just undergone a total hip arthroplasty (tha). which instruction is crucial for the nurse to include in the client's postoperative teaching to prevent hip dislocation?

choices.text: Use an abduction pillow between the legs, especially when turning.
Cross the legs only when sitting in a chair.
Elevate the affected leg when sitting.
Avoid extending the hip beyond neutral position.

questions.rationale: Maintaining hip abduction (keeping the legs apart) is critical after THA to prevent dislocation of the new joint, especially with a posterior approach. An abduction pillow (C) helps maintain this position, particularly during turning or while in bed. Hip flexion beyond 90 degrees, adduction (crossing legs - B), and internal rotation should be avoided. Extending the hip (A) is generally not restricted. Elevating the leg (D) helps with edema but doesn't directly prevent dislocation. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing (Respiratory)
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client with pneumonia has a nursing diagnosis of ineffective airway clearance. which nursing intervention is most appropriate to help liquefy respiratory secretions?

choices.text: Positioning the client flat in bed.

questions.rationale: Adequate hydration helps to thin and liquefy respiratory secretions, making them easier to expectorate (cough up). Encouraging fluid intake (unless contraindicated) is a key intervention for ineffective airway clearance (B). Cough suppressants (A) should generally be avoided unless the cough is nonproductive and exhausting, as coughing helps clear secretions. Positioning flat (C) can hinder lung expansion; Fowler's or Semi-Fowler's is preferred. Limiting activity (D) may be necessary based on oxygenation, but mobilization actually helps mobilize secretions. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Catheter Care
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with a urinary catheter should have their intake and output monitored closely. why is this important?
questions.rationale: Accurate I&O monitoring is crucial for all three reasons. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: COPD Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (copd) is experiencing increased shortness of breath and wheezing. which assessment finding would require immediate attention?

choices.text: Use of accessory muscles for breathing

questions.rationale: An oxygen saturation of 88% indicates hypoxemia, a life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. The other findings are concerning but not as immediately life-threatening. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Dehydration Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with severe dehydration would likely exhibit which of the following symptoms?
questions.rationale: Dehydration leads to decreased blood volume, resulting in hypotension. Bradycardia, edema, and bounding pulses are not associated with dehydration. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Fluid Volume Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with severe dehydration would likely exhibit which of the following symptoms?
questions.rationale: Dehydration leads to decreased blood volume, resulting in hypotension. Bradycardia, edema, and bounding pulses are not associated with dehydration. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Intracranial Pressure
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with a head injury is exhibiting signs of increased intracranial pressure (icp). which of the following are indicative of increased icp? select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Increased ICP typically presents with changes in level of consciousness, widening pulse pressure (Cushing's triad), irregular respirations (Cheyne-Stokes), and hypertension. Bradycardia and pinpoint pupils are not typical signs of increased ICP. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Nasogastric Tube Management
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with a nasogastric (ng) tube is experiencing nausea and vomiting. what is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Increase the rate of NG tube feeding.
Administer an antiemetic medication as ordered.
Encourage the patient to drink clear liquids.
Check the placement of the NG tube.

questions.rationale: Checking NG tube placement is the priority to ensure that the feeding is not entering the lungs. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Neurological Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with a head injury is exhibiting signs of increased intracranial pressure (icp). which of the following are indicative of increased icp? select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Increased ICP typically presents with changes in level of consciousness, widening pulse pressure (Cushing's triad), irregular respirations (Cheyne-Stokes), and hypertension. Bradycardia and pinpoint pupils are not typical signs of increased ICP. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: NG Tube Management
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with a nasogastric (ng) tube is experiencing nausea and vomiting. what is the priority nursing action?

choices.text: Increase the rate of NG tube feeding.
Check the placement of the NG tube.
Encourage the patient to drink clear liquids.
Administer an antiemetic medication as ordered.

questions.rationale: Checking NG tube placement is the priority to ensure that the feeding is not entering the lungs. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Oxygen Therapy
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient is receiving oxygen via a nasal cannula. what is the maximum flow rate typically used with a nasal cannula?
questions.rationale: Higher flow rates with nasal cannulas can cause nasal dryness and irritation, and may not increase oxygen saturation significantly. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pain Management
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient reports a pain level of 8/10 after surgery. which of the following is the most appropriate initial action?

choices.text: Teach the patient about nonpharmacological pain management techniques.
Reassess the patient's pain in 30 minutes.
Tell the patient to try to tough it out.

questions.rationale: Given the high pain level, administering the prescribed analgesic is the most appropriate initial action. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pain Management in Post-Op
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a postoperative client reports pain of 7/10 despite receiving the prescribed opioid analgesic 2 hours ago. which nursing interventions are appropriate? select all that apply.

choices.text: Administer the prescribed PRN opioid immediately
Assess the client's pain using a comprehensive pain assessment tool
Encourage the client to wait until the next scheduled dose

questions.rationale: Appropriate interventions include notifying the provider about inadequate pain control, performing a comprehensive pain assessment, and offering non-pharmacological measures. The nurse should not administer another dose if it's too early per orders, and should not encourage the client to simply endure uncontrolled pain. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Respiratory Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (copd) is experiencing increased shortness of breath and wheezing. which assessment finding would require immediate attention?

choices.text: Use of accessory muscles for breathing

questions.rationale: An oxygen saturation of 88% indicates hypoxemia, a life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. The other findings are concerning but not as immediately life-threatening. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Respiratory Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client has crackles in the lung bases. which position should the nurse place the client in to facilitate breathing?
questions.rationale: High Fowler's position (sitting upright at a 60-90 degree angle) allows for maximum lung expansion, especially in the bases, which helps mobilize secretions and improve ventilation in clients with crackles. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Thyroid Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, signs and symptoms of hyperthyroidism include: select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Hyperthyroidism causes increased metabolism, leading to weight loss, heat intolerance, and tremors. A goiter may also be present. Bradycardia and constipation are associated with hypothyroidism. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Urinary Catheter Care
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with a urinary catheter should have their intake and output monitored closely. why is this important?
questions.rationale: Accurate I&O monitoring is crucial for all three reasons. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Wound Care
questions.text: Based on current best practices, which of the following is the best method for cleaning a wound?
questions.rationale: Sterile saline solution is the most appropriate solution for wound cleaning to minimize tissue damage and infection risk. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Wound Management
questions.text: Based on current best practices, what is the best way to clean a new wound?

choices.text: Leave the wound uncovered to air dry.

questions.rationale: Soap and water are the best way to clean a new wound to minimize infection. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with chronic kidney disease is receiving hemodialysis. which assessment finding should the nurse report to the provider immediately?

choices.text: A weight gain of 1.5 kg since the last dialysis session
Mild itching at the arteriovenous fistula site

questions.rationale: A potassium level of 6.8 mEq/L indicates severe hyperkalemia, which is a life-threatening condition that can lead to cardiac arrhythmias and requires immediate intervention. While elevated blood pressure (A), weight gain (B), and mild itching (D) are concerning, they are not as immediately life-threatening as severe hyperkalemia. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a patient with cirrhosis who develops ascites. which nursing intervention would be most appropriate for this patient?

choices.text: Restricting fluids and monitoring daily weights
Positioning the patient flat in bed to redistribute fluid

questions.rationale: For patients with ascites due to cirrhosis, fluid restriction and monitoring daily weights are appropriate interventions to manage fluid accumulation. A high-sodium diet (A) would worsen fluid retention. Positioning flat (C) could increase respiratory distress due to pressure on the diaphragm. Antibiotics (D) are not routinely used for ascites unless there is a specific infection. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient has been prescribed furosemide (lasix) for heart failure. which electrolyte should the nurse monitor most closely?
questions.rationale: Furosemide (Lasix) is a loop diuretic that causes increased excretion of potassium, which can lead to hypokalemia. Potassium levels must be monitored closely as hypokalemia can cause cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, and other complications. While furosemide also affects sodium (A), calcium (C), and magnesium (D) levels, potassium depletion is the most common and potentially dangerous electrolyte imbalance. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client admitted with heart failure reports shortness of breath when lying flat. the nurse documents this finding as:
questions.rationale: Orthopnea (B) is the term specifically used to describe difficulty breathing when lying flat, which is relieved by sitting or standing up. It's a common symptom of left-sided heart failure due to increased pulmonary venous pressure and congestion when supine. Dyspnea on exertion (A) is shortness of breath with activity. Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (C) is sudden awakening at night with shortness of breath. Tachypnea (D) is rapid breathing. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a client with suspected right-sided heart failure. which clinical manifestations would support this diagnosis? (select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Right-sided heart failure results in systemic venous congestion. Manifestations include JVD (B) due to increased pressure in the superior vena cava, dependent edema (C) in the lower extremities or sacrum, ascites (E - fluid in the peritoneal cavity), and enlargement of the liver and spleen (hepatosplenomegaly) (F) due to venous engorgement. Crackles (A) and orthopnea (D) are primarily associated with left-sided heart failure due to pulmonary congestion. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client presents to the emergency department with severe chest pain described as crushing and radiating to the left arm. an ecg shows st-segment elevation. which condition should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: The classic presentation of severe, crushing chest pain, often radiating, combined with ST-segment elevation on the ECG is highly indicative of an ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) (C). This signifies transmural myocardial injury requiring immediate reperfusion therapy. Stable angina (A) is typically relieved by rest or nitroglycerin. Pericarditis (B) often presents with pleuritic chest pain relieved by leaning forward and may show diffuse ST elevation. Aortic dissection (D) typically causes tearing/ripping pain that radiates to the back. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, which cardiac biomarker is the most sensitive and specific indicator of myocardial damage?
questions.rationale: Cardiac troponins (Troponin I and Troponin T) (D) are proteins found in heart muscle that are released into the bloodstream when there is myocardial injury, such as during an MI. They are highly specific to cardiac muscle and remain elevated longer than other biomarkers, making them the preferred indicators for diagnosing MI. CK (A) and specifically CK-MB are less specific as they can be elevated with skeletal muscle injury. Myoglobin (B) rises early but is not cardiac-specific. LDH (C) is non-specific and has largely been replaced by more specific markers. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client is prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin for angina. the nurse instructs the client to take one tablet under the tongue at the onset of chest pain and, if pain persists, repeat the dose every 5 minutes for a maximum of ______ doses.
questions.rationale: Standard instructions for sublingual nitroglycerin for acute angina are to take one dose immediately. If pain is not relieved within 5 minutes, a second dose can be taken. If pain still persists after another 5 minutes, a third dose can be taken. If pain continues after three doses (total), the client should seek emergency medical attention immediately as this may indicate an MI. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is monitoring a client receiving a heparin infusion. the client's activated partial thromboplastin time (aptt) is 110 seconds (control value 25-35 seconds). what is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.
Continue the infusion at the current rate.

questions.rationale: The therapeutic range for aPTT during heparin therapy is typically 1.5 to 2.5 times the control value (approximately 40-85 seconds in this case). An aPTT of 110 seconds is significantly elevated above the therapeutic range, indicating excessive anticoagulation and placing the client at high risk for bleeding. The priority action is to stop the infusion immediately and notify the healthcare provider (C). Increasing the rate (A) would worsen the situation. Continuing the current rate (B) would maintain the excessive anticoagulation. Vitamin K (D) antagonizes warfarin, not heparin (protamine sulfate is heparin's antidote). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is teaching a client newly diagnosed with hypertension about lifestyle modifications. which recommendations should be included? (select all that apply.)

choices.text: Adopt the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet.

questions.rationale: Lifestyle modifications are crucial for managing hypertension. Key recommendations include adopting the DASH diet (A - rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat dairy), limiting sodium intake (B - usually <1500-2300 mg/day), engaging in regular aerobic exercise (D - e.g., brisk walking for at least 150 minutes/week), and limiting alcohol consumption (E - ≤1 drink/day for women, ≤2 drinks/day for men). Increasing saturated fats (C) contributes to atherosclerosis and can worsen cardiovascular health. Caffeine (F) can cause temporary BP spikes, so limiting (not increasing) intake may be advised. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse observes the following rhythm on a client's cardiac monitor: irregular rhythm, no discernible p waves, fibrillatory waves present, ventricular rate of 130 bpm. this rhythm is identified as:
questions.rationale: Atrial fibrillation (B) is characterized by chaotic atrial activity (fibrillatory waves instead of P waves), resulting in an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm. The atrial rate is typically >350 bpm (not measurable), and the ventricular rate varies depending on AV node conduction (in this case, 130 bpm, indicating rapid ventricular response). Sinus tachycardia (A) has regular rhythm with P waves preceding each QRS. Ventricular tachycardia (C) originates in the ventricles and has wide QRS complexes. Atrial flutter (D) has distinct, regular 'sawtooth' flutter waves (usually 250-350 bpm), often with regular ventricular response. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, what is the primary purpose of administering aspirin to a client experiencing an acute myocardial infarction (mi)?

choices.text: To dissolve the existing coronary thrombus.

questions.rationale: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent. In the context of an acute MI, which is usually caused by thrombus formation on a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque, aspirin works by inhibiting platelet aggregation (clumping), thereby helping to prevent further clot formation and extension (C). Thrombolytics or PCI are used to dissolve/remove the existing clot (A). Nitroglycerin and morphine relieve pain (B). Beta-blockers reduce oxygen demand (D). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, when assessing a client's peripheral pulses, a finding described as '2+' typically indicates a pulse that is _________ and easily palpable.
questions.rationale: Peripheral pulses are often graded on a scale (e.g., 0 to 3+ or 0 to 4+). A common scale is: 0 = Absent, 1+ = Weak/Thready, 2+ = Normal/Easily Palpable, 3+ = Bounding. Therefore, a 2+ pulse is considered the expected, normal finding. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Angina Management
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient experiencing angina reports chest pain radiating to the jaw and left arm. what is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Assess vital signs and oxygen saturation.
Instruct the patient to take deep breaths.

questions.rationale: While all options are appropriate, assessing vital signs and oxygen saturation is the immediate priority to determine the severity of the situation and guide further interventions. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Blood Transfusion
questions.text: Based on current best practices, which of the following are appropriate nursing interventions during a blood transfusion? select all that apply.

choices.text: Monitor vital signs every 15 minutes for the first hour.
Administer the blood with 5% dextrose solution.
Slow the infusion if the patient develops a fever.

questions.rationale: Blood should never be administered with dextrose solutions as they can cause hemolysis of red blood cells. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiac Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient experiencing angina reports chest pain radiating to the jaw and left arm. what is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Assess vital signs and oxygen saturation.
Instruct the patient to take deep breaths.

questions.rationale: While all options are appropriate, assessing vital signs and oxygen saturation is the immediate priority to determine the severity of the situation and guide further interventions. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Hypertension Management
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient has a blood pressure of 160/95 mmhg. what is the priority intervention?

choices.text: Place the patient in a supine position.
Reassess the blood pressure in 5 minutes.

questions.rationale: Reassessing the blood pressure to confirm the reading is the priority intervention before taking further action. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with acute coronary syndrome is experiencing chest pain. which medication should the nurse administer first?
questions.rationale: Aspirin should be administered first in acute coronary syndrome (ACS) to inhibit platelet aggregation and potentially limit the size of the developing thrombus. The American Heart Association recommends immediate administration of aspirin for patients with suspected ACS. Nitroglycerin (B) relieves pain but doesn't affect outcomes as significantly. Metoprolol (A) and morphine (C) are important but secondary to aspirin. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with heart failure has been prescribed an ace inhibitor. which assessment finding indicates a potential adverse effect of this medication?
questions.rationale: A persistent dry cough is a common side effect of ACE inhibitors due to the accumulation of bradykinin in the lungs. This side effect may require medication adjustment or change to an ARB. A productive cough (A) suggests an infection rather than a medication side effect. Increased urine output (C) and decreased heart rate (D) are expected therapeutic effects, not adverse effects. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a patient with a diagnosis of unstable angina. which ecg change would the nurse expect to see during an anginal episode?
questions.rationale: ST-segment depression is the classic ECG finding during an episode of unstable angina, indicating subendocardial ischemia. ST-segment elevation (A) typically indicates myocardial infarction with full-thickness damage. Q waves (C) represent tissue death after MI. Prolonged PR interval (D) is associated with conduction delays, not ischemia. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a client admitted with heart failure reports shortness of breath when lying flat. the nurse documents this finding as:
questions.rationale: Orthopnea (B) is the term specifically used to describe difficulty breathing when lying flat, which is relieved by sitting or standing up. It's a common symptom of left-sided heart failure due to increased pulmonary venous pressure and congestion when supine. Dyspnea on exertion (A) is shortness of breath with activity. Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (C) is sudden awakening at night with shortness of breath. Tachypnea (D) is rapid breathing. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a nurse is assessing a client with suspected right-sided heart failure. which clinical manifestations would support this diagnosis? (select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Right-sided heart failure results in systemic venous congestion. Manifestations include JVD (B) due to increased pressure in the superior vena cava, dependent edema (C) in the lower extremities or sacrum, ascites (E - fluid in the peritoneal cavity), and enlargement of the liver and spleen (hepatosplenomegaly) (F) due to venous engorgement. Crackles (A) and orthopnea (D) are primarily associated with left-sided heart failure due to pulmonary congestion. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a client presents to the emergency department with severe chest pain described as crushing and radiating to the left arm. an ecg shows st-segment elevation. which condition should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: The classic presentation of severe, crushing chest pain, often radiating, combined with ST-segment elevation on the ECG is highly indicative of an ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) (C). This signifies transmural myocardial injury requiring immediate reperfusion therapy. Stable angina (A) is typically relieved by rest or nitroglycerin. Pericarditis (B) often presents with pleuritic chest pain relieved by leaning forward and may show diffuse ST elevation. Aortic dissection (D) typically causes tearing/ripping pain that radiates to the back. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, which cardiac biomarker is the most sensitive and specific indicator of myocardial damage?
questions.rationale: Cardiac troponins (Troponin I and Troponin T) (D) are proteins found in heart muscle that are released into the bloodstream when there is myocardial injury, such as during an MI. They are highly specific to cardiac muscle and remain elevated longer than other biomarkers, making them the preferred indicators for diagnosing MI. CK (A) and specifically CK-MB are less specific as they can be elevated with skeletal muscle injury. Myoglobin (B) rises early but is not cardiac-specific. LDH (C) is non-specific and has largely been replaced by more specific markers. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a client is prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin for angina. the nurse instructs the client to take one tablet under the tongue at the onset of chest pain and, if pain persists, repeat the dose every 5 minutes for a maximum of ______ doses.
questions.rationale: Standard instructions for sublingual nitroglycerin for acute angina are to take one dose immediately. If pain is not relieved within 5 minutes, a second dose can be taken. If pain still persists after another 5 minutes, a third dose can be taken. If pain continues after three doses (total), the client should seek emergency medical attention immediately as this may indicate an MI. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a nurse is monitoring a client receiving a heparin infusion. the client's activated partial thromboplastin time (aptt) is 110 seconds (control value 25-35 seconds). what is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.
Continue the infusion at the current rate.

questions.rationale: The therapeutic range for aPTT during heparin therapy is typically 1.5 to 2.5 times the control value (approximately 40-85 seconds in this case). An aPTT of 110 seconds is significantly elevated above the therapeutic range, indicating excessive anticoagulation and placing the client at high risk for bleeding. The priority action is to stop the infusion immediately and notify the healthcare provider (C). Increasing the rate (A) would worsen the situation. Continuing the current rate (B) would maintain the excessive anticoagulation. Vitamin K (D) antagonizes warfarin, not heparin (protamine sulfate is heparin's antidote). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a nurse is teaching a client newly diagnosed with hypertension about lifestyle modifications. which recommendations should be included? (select all that apply.)

choices.text: Adopt the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet.

questions.rationale: Lifestyle modifications are crucial for managing hypertension. Key recommendations include adopting the DASH diet (A - rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat dairy), limiting sodium intake (B - usually <1500-2300 mg/day), engaging in regular aerobic exercise (D - e.g., brisk walking for at least 150 minutes/week), and limiting alcohol consumption (E - ≤1 drink/day for women, ≤2 drinks/day for men). Increasing saturated fats (C) contributes to atherosclerosis and can worsen cardiovascular health. Caffeine (F) can cause temporary BP spikes, so limiting (not increasing) intake may be advised. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a nurse observes the following rhythm on a client's cardiac monitor: irregular rhythm, no discernible p waves, fibrillatory waves present, ventricular rate of 130 bpm. this rhythm is identified as:
questions.rationale: Atrial fibrillation (B) is characterized by chaotic atrial activity (fibrillatory waves instead of P waves), resulting in an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm. The atrial rate is typically >350 bpm (not measurable), and the ventricular rate varies depending on AV node conduction (in this case, 130 bpm, indicating rapid ventricular response). Sinus tachycardia (A) has regular rhythm with P waves preceding each QRS. Ventricular tachycardia (C) originates in the ventricles and has wide QRS complexes. Atrial flutter (D) has distinct, regular 'sawtooth' flutter waves (usually 250-350 bpm), often with regular ventricular response. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, what is the primary purpose of administering aspirin to a client experiencing an acute myocardial infarction (mi)?

choices.text: To dissolve the existing coronary thrombus.

questions.rationale: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent. In the context of an acute MI, which is usually caused by thrombus formation on a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque, aspirin works by inhibiting platelet aggregation (clumping), thereby helping to prevent further clot formation and extension (C). Thrombolytics or PCI are used to dissolve/remove the existing clot (A). Nitroglycerin and morphine relieve pain (B). Beta-blockers reduce oxygen demand (D). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, when assessing a client's peripheral pulses, a finding described as '2+' typically indicates a pulse that is _________ and easily palpable.
questions.rationale: Peripheral pulses are often graded on a scale (e.g., 0 to 3+ or 0 to 4+). A common scale is: 0 = Absent, 1+ = Weak/Thready, 2+ = Normal/Easily Palpable, 3+ = Bounding. Therefore, a 2+ pulse is considered the expected, normal finding. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Angina Management
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a patient experiencing angina reports chest pain radiating to the jaw and left arm. what is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Instruct the patient to take deep breaths.
Assess vital signs and oxygen saturation.

questions.rationale: While all options are appropriate, assessing vital signs and oxygen saturation is the immediate priority to determine the severity of the situation and guide further interventions. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Blood Transfusion
questions.text: According to nursing standards, which of the following are appropriate nursing interventions during a blood transfusion? select all that apply.

choices.text: Monitor vital signs every 15 minutes for the first hour.
Administer the blood with 5% dextrose solution.
Slow the infusion if the patient develops a fever.

questions.rationale: Blood should never be administered with dextrose solutions as they can cause hemolysis of red blood cells. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiac Assessment
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a patient experiencing angina reports chest pain radiating to the jaw and left arm. what is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Assess vital signs and oxygen saturation.
Instruct the patient to take deep breaths.

questions.rationale: While all options are appropriate, assessing vital signs and oxygen saturation is the immediate priority to determine the severity of the situation and guide further interventions. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Hypertension Management
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a patient has a blood pressure of 160/95 mmhg. what is the priority intervention?

choices.text: Reassess the blood pressure in 5 minutes.
Place the patient in a supine position.

questions.rationale: Reassessing the blood pressure to confirm the reading is the priority intervention before taking further action. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a patient with acute coronary syndrome is experiencing chest pain. which medication should the nurse administer first?
questions.rationale: Aspirin should be administered first in acute coronary syndrome (ACS) to inhibit platelet aggregation and potentially limit the size of the developing thrombus. The American Heart Association recommends immediate administration of aspirin for patients with suspected ACS. Nitroglycerin (B) relieves pain but doesn't affect outcomes as significantly. Metoprolol (A) and morphine (C) are important but secondary to aspirin. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: According to nursing standards, a patient with heart failure has been prescribed an ace inhibitor. which assessment finding indicates a potential adverse effect of this medication?
questions.rationale: A persistent dry cough is a common side effect of ACE inhibitors due to the accumulation of bradykinin in the lungs. This side effect may require medication adjustment or change to an ARB. A productive cough (A) suggests an infection rather than a medication side effect. Increased urine output (C) and decreased heart rate (D) are expected therapeutic effects, not adverse effects. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a pregnant patient's last menstrual period (lmp) began on june 10th. using nägele's rule, what is the estimated date of delivery (edd)?
questions.rationale: Nägele's rule is calculated by subtracting 3 months from the first day of the LMP and adding 7 days. LMP: June 10th. Subtract 3 months: March 10th. Add 7 days: March 17th. Therefore, the EDD is March 17th. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a patient in the third trimester of pregnancy. which physiological change is considered normal during this period?

choices.text: Increase in respiratory rate and depth.
Decrease in heart rate by 10 bpm.

questions.rationale: During pregnancy, particularly the third trimester, progesterone causes increased sensitivity of the respiratory center to CO2 and maternal oxygen demands increase, leading to increased respiratory rate and tidal volume (depth) (B). Total blood volume significantly increases (A). Heart rate typically increases by 10-15 bpm (C). Dependent edema often worsens in the third trimester due to pressure from the enlarging uterus on pelvic veins and increased fluid volume (D). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is educating a pregnant client about danger signs that require immediate reporting to the healthcare provider. which signs should be included? (select all that apply.)

choices.text: Mild ankle edema at the end of the day
Sudden gush of fluid from the vagina
Occasional nausea in the first trimester
Absence of fetal movement for >12 hours

questions.rationale: Vaginal bleeding (A) can indicate issues like placenta previa or abruption. Sudden gush of fluid (C) suggests rupture of membranes. Severe headache (D) can be a sign of preeclampsia. Decreased or absent fetal movement (F) can indicate fetal distress. Mild ankle edema (B) is common and usually benign. Occasional nausea (E) is typical morning sickness. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient in active labor has ruptured membranes. the nurse notes a baseline fetal heart rate (fhr) of 135 bpm. during a contraction, the fhr drops abruptly to 90 bpm and returns quickly to baseline after the contraction ends. what is the most likely cause of this fhr pattern?
questions.rationale: The FHR pattern described is characteristic of variable decelerations, which are caused by umbilical cord compression (B). Key features include: abrupt onset, rapid drop (often below 100 bpm), variable timing in relation to contractions, and rapid return to baseline. Variable decelerations have a distinctive 'V' or 'U' shape on the monitor. Uteroplacental insufficiency (A) causes late decelerations, which have a gradual onset and recovery, occur after the peak of the contraction, and have a uniform shape. Maternal hypotension (C) typically causes prolonged decelerations. Fetal head compression (D) causes early decelerations, which mirror the contraction and rarely drop below 100 bpm. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a laboring client's membranes rupture spontaneously. the nurse notes the amniotic fluid is greenish-brown. what is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Perform a vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation.
Assess the fetal heart rate (FHR) and pattern.
Administer oxygen to the mother via non-rebreather mask.

questions.rationale: Greenish-brown amniotic fluid indicates the presence of meconium, which can be a sign of fetal distress (hypoxia) either past or present. Meconium aspiration syndrome is a potential complication for the newborn. The immediate priority is to assess fetal well-being by evaluating the FHR and pattern (C) for signs of distress (e.g., bradycardia, late decelerations, decreased variability). While notifying the provider (D) and potentially administering oxygen (A) are important, assessing the FHR takes precedence. A vaginal examination (B) is contraindicated when membranes are ruptured and meconium is present due to increased infection risk and potential to advance cord/meconium. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a postpartum client 24 hours after a vaginal delivery. the nurse palpates the uterine fundus. where should the nurse expect to locate the fundus?

choices.text: Midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis
At the level of the symphysis pubis

questions.rationale: After delivery, the uterus begins the process of involution. By 24 hours postpartum, the fundus should typically be firm and located approximately 1 cm (or one fingerbreadth) below the umbilicus (B). It continues to descend about 1 cm per day, becoming non-palpable abdominally by around day 10-14. Locating it at the symphysis (A) occurs much later. Midway (C) would be around day 6. Above the umbilicus (D) immediately post-delivery is possible, but it should be below the umbilicus by 24 hours. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, the thinning and shortening of the cervix during the first stage of labor is referred to as ____________.
questions.rationale: Effacement describes the process where the cervix, normally about 2-3 cm long, progressively thins out and shortens, drawing up into the lower uterine segment. It is measured in percentages (0% to 100%). Dilation refers to the opening of the cervical os. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Postpartum Assessment
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a client 4 hours after vaginal delivery. which findings should the nurse report to the healthcare provider? select all that apply.

choices.text: Urinary output of 100 mL in 4 hours

questions.rationale: Decreased urinary output, elevated temperature, and elevated blood pressure require immediate provider notification as they may indicate complications like infection or preeclampsia. Lochia rubra with small clots and a firm, midline fundus are normal findings in the immediate postpartum period. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a woman in the first stage of labor. which finding indicates progression to the active phase?
questions.rationale: According to current labor guidelines, the active phase of labor begins at 6 cm dilation. 30% effacement (B) is insufficient for active labor. Station -3 (C) indicates the fetal head is still high in the pelvis. Contractions every 15 minutes (D) are too far apart for active labor, which typically has contractions every 2-3 minutes. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a pregnant woman at 34 weeks gestation presents with sudden onset of severe right upper quadrant pain, headache, and visual disturbances. her blood pressure is 168/110 mmhg. what is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: The symptoms of severe right upper quadrant pain (liver involvement), headache, visual disturbances, and severely elevated blood pressure (≥160/110 mmHg) are diagnostic criteria for preeclampsia with severe features. Gestational diabetes (A) presents with glucose intolerance. Placenta previa (C) presents with painless vaginal bleeding. While HELLP syndrome (D) is a complication of preeclampsia, the symptoms described more directly point to preeclampsia with severe features. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a 1-hour-old newborn. which finding would require immediate intervention?

choices.text: Respiratory rate of 68 breaths per minute
Heart rate of 110 beats per minute

questions.rationale: Central cyanosis (blue discoloration of the trunk, lips, and mucous membranes) indicates significant hypoxemia and requires immediate intervention. Acrocyanosis (A) is a normal finding in newborns, characterized by bluish extremities with normal core coloration. A heart rate of 110 bpm (B) is within normal range (100-160 bpm). A respiratory rate of 68 breaths per minute (C) is slightly elevated but can be normal in the first hours of life (normal range 40-60). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.text: According to nursing standards, a pregnant patient's last menstrual period (lmp) began on june 10th. using nägele's rule, what is the estimated date of delivery (edd)?

This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: In a hospital setting, a pregnant woman at 34 weeks gestation presents with sudden onset of severe right upper quadrant pain, headache, and visual disturbances. her blood pressure is 168/110 mmhg. what is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: The symptoms of severe right upper quadrant pain (liver involvement), headache, visual disturbances, and severely elevated blood pressure (≥160/110 mmHg) are diagnostic criteria for preeclampsia with severe features. Gestational diabetes (A) presents with glucose intolerance. Placenta previa (C) presents with painless vaginal bleeding. While HELLP syndrome (D) is a complication of preeclampsia, the symptoms described more directly point to preeclampsia with severe features. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: In a hospital setting, a nurse is assessing a 1-hour-old newborn. which finding would require immediate intervention?

choices.text: Respiratory rate of 68 breaths per minute
Heart rate of 110 beats per minute

questions.rationale: Central cyanosis (blue discoloration of the trunk, lips, and mucous membranes) indicates significant hypoxemia and requires immediate intervention. Acrocyanosis (A) is a normal finding in newborns, characterized by bluish extremities with normal core coloration. A heart rate of 110 bpm (B) is within normal range (100-160 bpm). A respiratory rate of 68 breaths per minute (C) is slightly elevated but can be normal in the first hours of life (normal range 40-60). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: During your clinical rotation, a pregnant patient's last menstrual period (lmp) began on june 10th. using nägele's rule, what is the estimated date of delivery (edd)?
questions.rationale: Nägele's rule is calculated by subtracting 3 months from the first day of the LMP and adding 7 days. LMP: June 10th. Subtract 3 months: March 10th. Add 7 days: March 17th. Therefore, the EDD is March 17th. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: During your clinical rotation, a nurse is assessing a patient in the third trimester of pregnancy. which physiological change is considered normal during this period?

choices.text: Decrease in heart rate by 10 bpm.
Increase in respiratory rate and depth.

questions.rationale: During pregnancy, particularly the third trimester, progesterone causes increased sensitivity of the respiratory center to CO2 and maternal oxygen demands increase, leading to increased respiratory rate and tidal volume (depth) (B). Total blood volume significantly increases (A). Heart rate typically increases by 10-15 bpm (C). Dependent edema often worsens in the third trimester due to pressure from the enlarging uterus on pelvic veins and increased fluid volume (D). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: During your clinical rotation, a nurse is educating a pregnant client about danger signs that require immediate reporting to the healthcare provider. which signs should be included? (select all that apply.)

choices.text: Mild ankle edema at the end of the day
Sudden gush of fluid from the vagina
Occasional nausea in the first trimester
Absence of fetal movement for >12 hours

questions.rationale: Vaginal bleeding (A) can indicate issues like placenta previa or abruption. Sudden gush of fluid (C) suggests rupture of membranes. Severe headache (D) can be a sign of preeclampsia. Decreased or absent fetal movement (F) can indicate fetal distress. Mild ankle edema (B) is common and usually benign. Occasional nausea (E) is typical morning sickness. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: During your clinical rotation, a patient in active labor has ruptured membranes. the nurse notes a baseline fetal heart rate (fhr) of 135 bpm. during a contraction, the fhr drops abruptly to 90 bpm and returns quickly to baseline after the contraction ends. what is the most likely cause of this fhr pattern?
questions.rationale: The FHR pattern described is characteristic of variable decelerations, which are caused by umbilical cord compression (B). Key features include: abrupt onset, rapid drop (often below 100 bpm), variable timing in relation to contractions, and rapid return to baseline. Variable decelerations have a distinctive 'V' or 'U' shape on the monitor. Uteroplacental insufficiency (A) causes late decelerations, which have a gradual onset and recovery, occur after the peak of the contraction, and have a uniform shape. Maternal hypotension (C) typically causes prolonged decelerations. Fetal head compression (D) causes early decelerations, which mirror the contraction and rarely drop below 100 bpm. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: During your clinical rotation, a laboring client's membranes rupture spontaneously. the nurse notes the amniotic fluid is greenish-brown. what is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Assess the fetal heart rate (FHR) and pattern.
Administer oxygen to the mother via non-rebreather mask.
Perform a vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation.

questions.rationale: Greenish-brown amniotic fluid indicates the presence of meconium, which can be a sign of fetal distress (hypoxia) either past or present. Meconium aspiration syndrome is a potential complication for the newborn. The immediate priority is to assess fetal well-being by evaluating the FHR and pattern (C) for signs of distress (e.g., bradycardia, late decelerations, decreased variability). While notifying the provider (D) and potentially administering oxygen (A) are important, assessing the FHR takes precedence. A vaginal examination (B) is contraindicated when membranes are ruptured and meconium is present due to increased infection risk and potential to advance cord/meconium. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: During your clinical rotation, a nurse is assessing a postpartum client 24 hours after a vaginal delivery. the nurse palpates the uterine fundus. where should the nurse expect to locate the fundus?

choices.text: At the level of the symphysis pubis
Midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis

questions.rationale: After delivery, the uterus begins the process of involution. By 24 hours postpartum, the fundus should typically be firm and located approximately 1 cm (or one fingerbreadth) below the umbilicus (B). It continues to descend about 1 cm per day, becoming non-palpable abdominally by around day 10-14. Locating it at the symphysis (A) occurs much later. Midway (C) would be around day 6. Above the umbilicus (D) immediately post-delivery is possible, but it should be below the umbilicus by 24 hours. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to erikson's stages of psychosocial development, which developmental task is characteristic of the toddler age group (1-3 years)?
questions.rationale: Erikson's stage for toddlers (1-3 years) is Autonomy vs. Shame and Doubt (D). During this stage, toddlers focus on developing a sense of personal control over physical skills and a sense of independence. Trust vs. Mistrust (A) is infancy (birth-1 year). Initiative vs. Guilt (B) is preschool (3-6 years). Industry vs. Inferiority (C) is school-age (6-12 years). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a common childhood illness characterized by a high fever for several days followed by the appearance of a maculopapular rash after the fever breaks is known as roseola infantum, caused by human __________ 6 or 7.
questions.rationale: Roseola infantum (also called sixth disease or exanthem subitum) is typically caused by Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or less commonly HHV-7. The classic presentation involves 3-5 days of high fever which resolves abruptly, followed by the onset of a non-itchy, pinkish-red maculopapular rash that often starts on the trunk and spreads. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. which clinical sign would the nurse assess for that is characteristic of meningeal irritation?
questions.rationale: Meningeal irritation causes inflammation of the meninges, leading to specific signs. Brudzinski's sign is positive when passive flexion of the child's neck causes flexion of the hips and knees (C). Kernig's sign is positive when resistance or pain occurs upon extending the knee while the hip is flexed. Nuchal rigidity (stiff neck) is also common (B is incorrect). A negative Kernig's sign (A) indicates absence of that specific sign of irritation. A sunken fontanelle (D) suggests dehydration rather than meningitis, and by age 4, the fontanelles have typically closed. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected epiglottitis. which intervention is the highest priority?

choices.text: Placing the child in a mist tent
Avoiding agitating the child and inspecting the throat

questions.rationale: In suspected epiglottitis, a potentially life-threatening condition, the highest priority is to avoid agitating the child and NOT to inspect the throat, as this could precipitate complete airway obstruction. A controlled environment should be maintained and an airway team should be present before any examination. While intubation (C) may be necessary, it should be done in a controlled setting. Antibiotics (A) and a mist tent (D) are important but secondary to airway management. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is administering immunizations to a 6-month-old infant. the parent asks why multiple vaccines are given at once. the nurse's best response is:

choices.text: 'The immune system can respond to millions of antigens at once, so multiple vaccines are safe.'
'All of the above are accurate explanations.'
'If we delay vaccines, your child could contract serious diseases during the waiting period.'
'It saves time and reduces the number of office visits needed.'

questions.rationale: All three statements are accurate explanations for administering multiple vaccines at once. The immune system can effectively respond to multiple antigens simultaneously (B). Combining vaccines reduces the number of visits needed (A), and delaying vaccines leaves children vulnerable to preventable diseases (C). Providing comprehensive education helps address parental concerns about vaccination schedules. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a 3-year-old child is admitted with suspected intussusception. which assessment finding is most characteristic of this condition?
questions.rationale: Currant jelly-like stools (dark red, mucoid stools) are the classic sign of intussusception due to intestinal bleeding and mucus production where one segment of bowel telescopes into another. Projectile vomiting (A) is more characteristic of pyloric stenosis. Clay-colored stools (C) suggest biliary obstruction. Severe diarrhea with mucus (D) is more typical of infectious gastroenteritis. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

Combining vaccines reduces the number of visits needed (A), and delaying vaccines leaves children vulnerable to preventable diseases (C). Providing comprehensive education helps address parental concerns about vaccination schedules. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: A patient's family member asks: A 3-year-old child is admitted with suspected intussusception. Which assessment finding is most characteristic of this condition?
questions.rationale: Currant jelly-like stools (dark red, mucoid stools) are the classic sign of intussusception due to intestinal bleeding and mucus production where one segment of bowel telescopes into another. Projectile vomiting (A) is more characteristic of pyloric stenosis. Clay-colored stools (C) suggest biliary obstruction. Severe diarrhea with mucus (D) is more typical of infectious gastroenteritis. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: For a patient with multiple comorbidities, according to erikson's stages of psychosocial development, which developmental task is characteristic of the toddler age group (1-3 years)?
questions.rationale: Erikson's stage for toddlers (1-3 years) is Autonomy vs. Shame and Doubt (D). During this stage, toddlers focus on developing a sense of personal control over physical skills and a sense of independence. Trust vs. Mistrust (A) is infancy (birth-1 year). Initiative vs. Guilt (B) is preschool (3-6 years). Industry vs. Inferiority (C) is school-age (6-12 years). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: For a patient with multiple comorbidities, a common childhood illness characterized by a high fever for several days followed by the appearance of a maculopapular rash after the fever breaks is known as roseola infantum, caused by human __________ 6 or 7.
questions.rationale: Roseola infantum (also called sixth disease or exanthem subitum) is typically caused by Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or less commonly HHV-7. The classic presentation involves 3-5 days of high fever which resolves abruptly, followed by the onset of a non-itchy, pinkish-red maculopapular rash that often starts on the trunk and spreads. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: For a patient with multiple comorbidities, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. which clinical sign would the nurse assess for that is characteristic of meningeal irritation?
questions.rationale: Meningeal irritation causes inflammation of the meninges, leading to specific signs. Brudzinski's sign is positive when passive flexion of the child's neck causes flexion of the hips and knees (C). Kernig's sign is positive when resistance or pain occurs upon extending the knee while the hip is flexed. Nuchal rigidity (stiff neck) is also common (B is incorrect). A negative Kernig's sign (A) indicates absence of that specific sign of irritation. A sunken fontanelle (D) suggests dehydration rather than meningitis, and by age 4, the fontanelles have typically closed. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: For a patient with multiple comorbidities, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected epiglottitis. which intervention is the highest priority?

choices.text: Placing the child in a mist tent
Avoiding agitating the child and inspecting the throat

questions.rationale: In suspected epiglottitis, a potentially life-threatening condition, the highest priority is to avoid agitating the child and NOT to inspect the throat, as this could precipitate complete airway obstruction. A controlled environment should be maintained and an airway team should be present before any examination. While intubation (C) may be necessary, it should be done in a controlled setting. Antibiotics (A) and a mist tent (D) are important but secondary to airway management. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: For a patient with multiple comorbidities, a nurse is administering immunizations to a 6-month-old infant. the parent asks why multiple vaccines are given at once. the nurse's best response is:

choices.text: 'All of the above are accurate explanations.'
'It saves time and reduces the number of office visits needed.'
'If we delay vaccines, your child could contract serious diseases during the waiting period.'
'The immune system can respond to millions of antigens at once, so multiple vaccines are safe.'

questions.rationale: All three statements are accurate explanations for administering multiple vaccines at once. The immune system can effectively respond to multiple antigens simultaneously (B). Combining vaccines reduces the number of visits needed (A), and delaying vaccines leaves children vulnerable to preventable diseases (C). Providing comprehensive education helps address parental concerns about vaccination schedules. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.
