questions.rationale: Higher flow rates with nasal cannulas can cause nasal dryness and irritation, and may not increase oxygen saturation significantly. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in medical-surgical nursing.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a patient reports a pain level of 8/10 after surgery. which of the following is the most appropriate initial action?

choices.text: Tell the patient to try to tough it out.
Teach the patient about nonpharmacological pain management techniques.
Reassess the patient's pain in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Given the high pain level, administering the prescribed analgesic is the most appropriate initial action. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a postoperative client reports pain of 7/10 despite receiving the prescribed opioid analgesic 2 hours ago. which nursing interventions are appropriate? select all that apply.

choices.text: Administer the prescribed PRN opioid immediately
Assess the client's pain using a comprehensive pain assessment tool
Encourage the client to wait until the next scheduled dose

questions.rationale: Appropriate interventions include notifying the provider about inadequate pain control, performing a comprehensive pain assessment, and offering non-pharmacological measures. The nurse should not administer another dose if it's too early per orders, and should not encourage the client to simply endure uncontrolled pain. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a patient with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (copd) is experiencing increased shortness of breath and wheezing. which assessment finding would require immediate attention?

choices.text: Use of accessory muscles for breathing

questions.rationale: An oxygen saturation of 88% indicates hypoxemia, a life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. The other findings are concerning but not as immediately life-threatening. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a client has crackles in the lung bases. which position should the nurse place the client in to facilitate breathing?

choices.text: Prone position, in accordance with facility protocol
High Fowler's position, which is recommended for this situation
Lateral position, as indicated by assessment findings
Supine position, as indicated by assessment findings

questions.rationale: High Fowler's position (sitting upright at a 60-90 degree angle) allows for maximum lung expansion, especially in the bases, which helps mobilize secretions and improve ventilation in clients with crackles. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Understanding this concept is crucial for providing evidence-based care.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, signs and symptoms of hyperthyroidism include: select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Hyperthyroidism causes increased metabolism, leading to weight loss, heat intolerance, and tremors. A goiter may also be present. Bradycardia and constipation are associated with hypothyroidism. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a patient with a urinary catheter should have their intake and output monitored closely. why is this important?
questions.rationale: Accurate I&O monitoring is crucial for all three reasons. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, which of the following is the best method for cleaning a wound?
questions.rationale: Sterile saline solution is the most appropriate solution for wound cleaning to minimize tissue damage and infection risk. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, what is the best way to clean a new wound?

choices.text: Leave the wound uncovered to air dry.

questions.rationale: Soap and water are the best way to clean a new wound to minimize infection. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in medical-surgical nursing.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a patient with chronic kidney disease is receiving hemodialysis. which assessment finding should the nurse report to the provider immediately?

choices.text: Mild itching at the arteriovenous fistula site
A weight gain of 1.5 kg since the last dialysis session

questions.rationale: A potassium level of 6.8 mEq/L indicates severe hyperkalemia, which is a life-threatening condition that can lead to cardiac arrhythmias and requires immediate intervention. While elevated blood pressure (A), weight gain (B), and mild itching (D) are concerning, they are not as immediately life-threatening as severe hyperkalemia. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a patient with cirrhosis who develops ascites. which nursing intervention would be most appropriate for this patient?

choices.text: Restricting fluids and monitoring daily weights
Positioning the patient flat in bed to redistribute fluid

questions.rationale: For patients with ascites due to cirrhosis, fluid restriction and monitoring daily weights are appropriate interventions to manage fluid accumulation. A high-sodium diet (A) would worsen fluid retention. Positioning flat (C) could increase respiratory distress due to pressure on the diaphragm. Antibiotics (D) are not routinely used for ascites unless there is a specific infection. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a patient has been prescribed furosemide (lasix) for heart failure. which electrolyte should the nurse monitor most closely?
questions.rationale: Furosemide (Lasix) is a loop diuretic that causes increased excretion of potassium, which can lead to hypokalemia. Potassium levels must be monitored closely as hypokalemia can cause cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, and other complications. While furosemide also affects sodium (A), calcium (C), and magnesium (D) levels, potassium depletion is the most common and potentially dangerous electrolyte imbalance. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client admitted with heart failure reports shortness of breath when lying flat. The nurse documents this finding as:
questions.rationale: Orthopnea (B) is the term specifically used to describe difficulty breathing when lying flat, which is relieved by sitting or standing up. It's a common symptom of left-sided heart failure due to increased pulmonary venous pressure and congestion when supine. Dyspnea on exertion (A) is shortness of breath with activity. Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (C) is sudden awakening at night with shortness of breath. Tachypnea (D) is rapid breathing. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a client with suspected right-sided heart failure. Which clinical manifestations would support this diagnosis? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Right-sided heart failure results in systemic venous congestion. Manifestations include JVD (B) due to increased pressure in the superior vena cava, dependent edema (C) in the lower extremities or sacrum, ascites (E - fluid in the peritoneal cavity), and enlargement of the liver and spleen (hepatosplenomegaly) (F) due to venous engorgement. Crackles (A) and orthopnea (D) are primarily associated with left-sided heart failure due to pulmonary congestion. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client presents to the emergency department with severe chest pain described as crushing and radiating to the left arm. An ECG shows ST-segment elevation. Which condition should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: The classic presentation of severe, crushing chest pain, often radiating, combined with ST-segment elevation on the ECG is highly indicative of an ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) (C). This signifies transmural myocardial injury requiring immediate reperfusion therapy. Stable angina (A) is typically relieved by rest or nitroglycerin. Pericarditis (B) often presents with pleuritic chest pain relieved by leaning forward and may show diffuse ST elevation. Aortic dissection (D) typically causes tearing/ripping pain that radiates to the back. Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, which cardiovascular symptoms cardiac biomarker is the most sensitive and specific indicator of myocardial damage?

choices.text: Creatine kinase (CK), in accordance with facility protocol
Troponin I or Troponin T, as indicated by assessment findings

questions.rationale: Cardiac troponins (Troponin I and Troponin T) (D) are proteins found in heart muscle that are released into the bloodstream when there is myocardial injury, such as during an MI. They are highly specific to cardiac muscle and remain elevated longer than other biomarkers, making them the preferred indicators for diagnosing MI. CK (A) and specifically CK-MB are less specific as they can be elevated with skeletal muscle injury. Myoglobin (B) rises early but is not cardiac-specific. LDH (C) is non-specific and has largely been replaced by more specific markers. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client is prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin for angina. The nurse instructs the client to take one tablet under the tongue at the onset of chest pain and, if pain persists, repeat the dose every 5 minutes for a maximum of ______ doses.
questions.rationale: Standard instructions for sublingual nitroglycerin for acute angina are to take one dose immediately. If pain is not relieved within 5 minutes, a second dose can be taken. If pain still persists after another 5 minutes, a third dose can be taken. If pain continues after three doses (total), the client should seek emergency medical attention immediately as this may indicate an MI. This information guides proper clinical decision-making.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is monitoring a client receiving a heparin infusion. The client's activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) is 110 seconds (control value 25-35 seconds). Which cardiac monitoring is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Continue the infusion at the current rate.
Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

questions.rationale: The therapeutic range for aPTT during heparin therapy is typically 1.5 to 2.5 times the control value (approximately 40-85 seconds in this case). An aPTT of 110 seconds is significantly elevated above the therapeutic range, indicating excessive anticoagulation and placing the client at high risk for bleeding. The priority action is to stop the infusion immediately and notify the healthcare provider (C). Increasing the rate (A) would worsen the situation. Continuing the current rate (B) would maintain the excessive anticoagulation. Vitamin K (D) antagonizes warfarin, not heparin (protamine sulfate is heparin's antidote). Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is teaching a client newly diagnosed with hypertension about lifestyle modifications. Which cardiovascular symptoms recommendations should be included? (Select all that apply.)

choices.text: Adopt the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet.

questions.rationale: Lifestyle modifications are crucial for managing hypertension. Key recommendations include adopting the DASH diet (A - rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat dairy), limiting sodium intake (B - usually <1500-2300 mg/day), engaging in regular aerobic exercise (D - e.g., brisk walking for at least 150 minutes/week), and limiting alcohol consumption (E - ≤1 drink/day for women, ≤2 drinks/day for men). Increasing saturated fats (C) contributes to atherosclerosis and can worsen cardiovascular health. Caffeine (F) can cause temporary BP spikes, so limiting (not increasing) intake may be advised. This represents a key concept in cardiovascular nursing.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse observes the following rhythm on a client's cardiac monitor: irregular rhythm, no discernible P waves, fibrillatory waves present, ventricular rate of 130 bpm. This rhythm is identified as:
questions.rationale: Atrial fibrillation (B) is characterized by chaotic atrial activity (fibrillatory waves instead of P waves), resulting in an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm. The atrial rate is typically >350 bpm (not measurable), and the ventricular rate varies depending on AV node conduction (in this case, 130 bpm, indicating rapid ventricular response). Sinus tachycardia (A) has regular rhythm with P waves preceding each QRS. Ventricular tachycardia (C) originates in the ventricles and has wide QRS complexes. Atrial flutter (D) has distinct, regular 'sawtooth' flutter waves (usually 250-350 bpm), often with regular ventricular response. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, what is the primary purpose of administering aspirin to a client experiencing an acute myocardial infarction (MI)?

choices.text: To dissolve the existing coronary thrombus.

questions.rationale: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent. In the context of an acute MI, which is usually caused by thrombus formation on a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque, aspirin works by inhibiting platelet aggregation (clumping), thereby helping to prevent further clot formation and extension (C). Thrombolytics or PCI are used to dissolve/remove the existing clot (A). Nitroglycerin and morphine relieve pain (B). Beta-blockers reduce oxygen demand (D). This information guides proper clinical decision-making.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, when assessing a client's peripheral pulses, a finding described as '2+' typically indicates a pulse that is _________ and easily palpable.
questions.rationale: Peripheral pulses are often graded on a scale (e.g., 0 to 3+ or 0 to 4+). A common scale is: 0 = Absent, 1+ = Weak/Thready, 2+ = Normal/Easily Palpable, 3+ = Bounding. Therefore, a 2+ pulse is considered the expected, normal finding. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

Instruct the patient to take deep breaths.
Obtain a 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG)., which is recommended for this situation

questions.rationale: While all options are appropriate, assessing vital signs and oxygen saturation is the immediate priority to determine the severity of the situation and guide further interventions. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with acute coronary syndrome is experiencing chest pain. Which medication should the nurse administer first?

choices.text: Morphine sulfate, to promote patient safety

questions.rationale: Aspirin should be administered first in acute coronary syndrome (ACS) to inhibit platelet aggregation and potentially limit the size of the developing thrombus. The American Heart Association recommends immediate administration of aspirin for patients with suspected ACS. Nitroglycerin (B) relieves pain but doesn't affect outcomes as significantly. Metoprolol (A) and morphine (C) are important but secondary to aspirin. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with heart failure has been prescribed an ACE inhibitor. Which circulation assessment assessment finding indicates a potential adverse effect of this medication?
questions.rationale: A persistent dry cough is a common side effect of ACE inhibitors due to the accumulation of bradykinin in the lungs. This side effect may require medication adjustment or change to an ARB. A productive cough (A) suggests an infection rather than a medication side effect. Increased urine output (C) and decreased heart rate (D) are expected therapeutic effects, not adverse effects. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a patient with a diagnosis of unstable angina. Which ECG change would the nurse expect to see during an anginal episode?
questions.rationale: ST-segment depression is the classic ECG finding during an episode of unstable angina, indicating subendocardial ischemia. ST-segment elevation (A) typically indicates myocardial infarction with full-thickness damage. Q waves (C) represent tissue death after MI. Prolonged PR interval (D) is associated with conduction delays, not ischemia. This information guides proper clinical decision-making.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a patient with a diagnosis of unstable angina. which ecg change would the nurse expect to see during an anginal episode?
questions.rationale: ST-segment depression is the classic ECG finding during an episode of unstable angina, indicating subendocardial ischemia. ST-segment elevation (A) typically indicates myocardial infarction with full-thickness damage. Q waves (C) represent tissue death after MI. Prolonged PR interval (D) is associated with conduction delays, not ischemia. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Understanding this concept is crucial for providing evidence-based care.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a client admitted with heart failure reports shortness of breath when lying flat. the nurse documents this finding as:

choices.text: Orthopnea, as indicated by assessment findings
Dyspnea on exertion, which is recommended for this situation

questions.rationale: Orthopnea (B) is the term specifically used to describe difficulty breathing when lying flat, which is relieved by sitting or standing up. It's a common symptom of left-sided heart failure due to increased pulmonary venous pressure and congestion when supine. Dyspnea on exertion (A) is shortness of breath with activity. Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (C) is sudden awakening at night with shortness of breath. Tachypnea (D) is rapid breathing. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This information guides proper clinical decision-making.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is assessing a client with suspected right-sided heart failure. which clinical manifestations would support this diagnosis? (select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Right-sided heart failure results in systemic venous congestion. Manifestations include JVD (B) due to increased pressure in the superior vena cava, dependent edema (C) in the lower extremities or sacrum, ascites (E - fluid in the peritoneal cavity), and enlargement of the liver and spleen (hepatosplenomegaly) (F) due to venous engorgement. Crackles (A) and orthopnea (D) are primarily associated with left-sided heart failure due to pulmonary congestion. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in cardiovascular nursing.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a client presents to the emergency department with severe chest pain described as crushing and radiating to the left arm. an ecg shows st-segment elevation. which condition should the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: The classic presentation of severe, crushing chest pain, often radiating, combined with ST-segment elevation on the ECG is highly indicative of an ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) (C). This signifies transmural myocardial injury requiring immediate reperfusion therapy. Stable angina (A) is typically relieved by rest or nitroglycerin. Pericarditis (B) often presents with pleuritic chest pain relieved by leaning forward and may show diffuse ST elevation. Aortic dissection (D) typically causes tearing/ripping pain that radiates to the back. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, which cardiac biomarker is the most sensitive and specific indicator of myocardial damage?
questions.rationale: Cardiac troponins (Troponin I and Troponin T) (D) are proteins found in heart muscle that are released into the bloodstream when there is myocardial injury, such as during an MI. They are highly specific to cardiac muscle and remain elevated longer than other biomarkers, making them the preferred indicators for diagnosing MI. CK (A) and specifically CK-MB are less specific as they can be elevated with skeletal muscle injury. Myoglobin (B) rises early but is not cardiac-specific. LDH (C) is non-specific and has largely been replaced by more specific markers. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This information guides proper clinical decision-making.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a client is prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin for angina. the nurse instructs the client to take one tablet under the tongue at the onset of chest pain and, if pain persists, repeat the dose every 5 minutes for a maximum of ______ doses.
questions.rationale: Standard instructions for sublingual nitroglycerin for acute angina are to take one dose immediately. If pain is not relieved within 5 minutes, a second dose can be taken. If pain still persists after another 5 minutes, a third dose can be taken. If pain continues after three doses (total), the client should seek emergency medical attention immediately as this may indicate an MI. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is monitoring a client receiving a heparin infusion. the client's activated partial thromboplastin time (aptt) is 110 seconds (control value 25-35 seconds). what is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.
Continue the infusion at the current rate.

questions.rationale: The therapeutic range for aPTT during heparin therapy is typically 1.5 to 2.5 times the control value (approximately 40-85 seconds in this case). An aPTT of 110 seconds is significantly elevated above the therapeutic range, indicating excessive anticoagulation and placing the client at high risk for bleeding. The priority action is to stop the infusion immediately and notify the healthcare provider (C). Increasing the rate (A) would worsen the situation. Continuing the current rate (B) would maintain the excessive anticoagulation. Vitamin K (D) antagonizes warfarin, not heparin (protamine sulfate is heparin's antidote). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is teaching a client newly diagnosed with hypertension about lifestyle modifications. which recommendations should be included? (select all that apply.)

choices.text: Adopt the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet.

questions.rationale: Lifestyle modifications are crucial for managing hypertension. Key recommendations include adopting the DASH diet (A - rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat dairy), limiting sodium intake (B - usually <1500-2300 mg/day), engaging in regular aerobic exercise (D - e.g., brisk walking for at least 150 minutes/week), and limiting alcohol consumption (E - ≤1 drink/day for women, ≤2 drinks/day for men). Increasing saturated fats (C) contributes to atherosclerosis and can worsen cardiovascular health. Caffeine (F) can cause temporary BP spikes, so limiting (not increasing) intake may be advised. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse observes the following rhythm on a client's cardiac monitor: irregular rhythm, no discernible p waves, fibrillatory waves present, ventricular rate of 130 bpm. this rhythm is identified as:
questions.rationale: Atrial fibrillation (B) is characterized by chaotic atrial activity (fibrillatory waves instead of P waves), resulting in an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm. The atrial rate is typically >350 bpm (not measurable), and the ventricular rate varies depending on AV node conduction (in this case, 130 bpm, indicating rapid ventricular response). Sinus tachycardia (A) has regular rhythm with P waves preceding each QRS. Ventricular tachycardia (C) originates in the ventricles and has wide QRS complexes. Atrial flutter (D) has distinct, regular 'sawtooth' flutter waves (usually 250-350 bpm), often with regular ventricular response. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, what is the primary purpose of administering aspirin to a client experiencing an acute myocardial infarction (mi)?

choices.text: To dissolve the existing coronary thrombus.

questions.rationale: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent. In the context of an acute MI, which is usually caused by thrombus formation on a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque, aspirin works by inhibiting platelet aggregation (clumping), thereby helping to prevent further clot formation and extension (C). Thrombolytics or PCI are used to dissolve/remove the existing clot (A). Nitroglycerin and morphine relieve pain (B). Beta-blockers reduce oxygen demand (D). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, when assessing a client's peripheral pulses, a finding described as '2+' typically indicates a pulse that is _________ and easily palpable.
questions.rationale: Peripheral pulses are often graded on a scale (e.g., 0 to 3+ or 0 to 4+). A common scale is: 0 = Absent, 1+ = Weak/Thready, 2+ = Normal/Easily Palpable, 3+ = Bounding. Therefore, a 2+ pulse is considered the expected, normal finding. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Cardiovascular Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a patient experiencing angina reports chest pain radiating to the jaw and left arm. what is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Instruct the patient to take deep breaths.
Assess vital signs and oxygen saturation.

questions.rationale: While all options are appropriate, assessing vital signs and oxygen saturation is the immediate priority to determine the severity of the situation and guide further interventions. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Cardiovascular Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, which of the following are appropriate nursing interventions during a blood transfusion? select all that apply.

choices.text: Monitor vital signs every 15 minutes for the first hour.
Administer the blood with 5% dextrose solution.
Slow the infusion if the patient develops a fever.

questions.rationale: Blood should never be administered with dextrose solutions as they can cause hemolysis of red blood cells. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Understanding this concept is crucial for providing evidence-based care.

questions.title: Cardiovascular Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a patient experiencing angina reports chest pain radiating to the jaw and left arm. what is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.text: Assess vital signs and oxygen saturation.
Instruct the patient to take deep breaths.

questions.rationale: While all options are appropriate, assessing vital signs and oxygen saturation is the immediate priority to determine the severity of the situation and guide further interventions. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in cardiovascular nursing.

questions.title: Cardiovascular Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a patient has a blood pressure of 160/95 mmhg. what is the priority intervention?

choices.text: Reassess the blood pressure in 5 minutes.
Place the patient in a supine position.

questions.rationale: Reassessing the blood pressure to confirm the reading is the priority intervention before taking further action. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a patient with acute coronary syndrome is experiencing chest pain. which medication should the nurse administer first?

choices.text: Aspirin, as indicated by assessment findings

questions.rationale: Aspirin should be administered first in acute coronary syndrome (ACS) to inhibit platelet aggregation and potentially limit the size of the developing thrombus. The American Heart Association recommends immediate administration of aspirin for patients with suspected ACS. Nitroglycerin (B) relieves pain but doesn't affect outcomes as significantly. Metoprolol (A) and morphine (C) are important but secondary to aspirin. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Cardiovascular
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a patient with heart failure has been prescribed an ace inhibitor. which assessment finding indicates a potential adverse effect of this medication?
questions.rationale: A persistent dry cough is a common side effect of ACE inhibitors due to the accumulation of bradykinin in the lungs. This side effect may require medication adjustment or change to an ARB. A productive cough (A) suggests an infection rather than a medication side effect. Increased urine output (C) and decreased heart rate (D) are expected therapeutic effects, not adverse effects. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a pregnant patient's last menstrual period (LMP) began on June 10th. Using Nägele's rule, what is the estimated date of delivery (EDD)?
questions.rationale: Nägele's rule is calculated by subtracting 3 months from the first day of the LMP and adding 7 days. LMP: June 10th. Subtract 3 months: March 10th. Add 7 days: March 17th. Therefore, the EDD is March 17th. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a patient in the third trimester of pregnancy. Which labor progression physiological change is considered normal during this period?

choices.text: Increase in respiratory rate and depth.
Decrease in heart rate by 10 bpm.

questions.rationale: During pregnancy, particularly the third trimester, progesterone causes increased sensitivity of the respiratory center to CO2 and maternal oxygen demands increase, leading to increased respiratory rate and tidal volume (depth) (B). Total blood volume significantly increases (A). Heart rate typically increases by 10-15 bpm (C). Dependent edema often worsens in the third trimester due to pressure from the enlarging uterus on pelvic veins and increased fluid volume (D). This represents a key concept in maternity nursing.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is educating a pregnant client about danger signs that require immediate reporting to the healthcare provider. Which signs should be included? (Select all that apply.)

choices.text: Mild ankle edema at the end of the day
Sudden gush of fluid from the vagina
Occasional nausea in the first trimester
Absence of fetal movement for >12 hours

questions.rationale: Vaginal bleeding (A) can indicate issues like placenta previa or abruption. Sudden gush of fluid (C) suggests rupture of membranes. Severe headache (D) can be a sign of preeclampsia. Decreased or absent fetal movement (F) can indicate fetal distress. Mild ankle edema (B) is common and usually benign. Occasional nausea (E) is typical morning sickness. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient in active labor has ruptured membranes. The nurse notes a baseline fetal heart rate (FHR) of 135 bpm. During a contraction, the FHR drops abruptly to 90 bpm and returns quickly to baseline after the contraction ends. Which labor progression is the most likely cause of this FHR pattern?

choices.text: Fetal head compression, following best practice guidelines

questions.rationale: The FHR pattern described is characteristic of variable decelerations, which are caused by umbilical cord compression (B). Key features include: abrupt onset, rapid drop (often below 100 bpm), variable timing in relation to contractions, and rapid return to baseline. Variable decelerations have a distinctive 'V' or 'U' shape on the monitor. Uteroplacental insufficiency (A) causes late decelerations, which have a gradual onset and recovery, occur after the peak of the contraction, and have a uniform shape. Maternal hypotension (C) typically causes prolonged decelerations. Fetal head compression (D) causes early decelerations, which mirror the contraction and rarely drop below 100 bpm. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a laboring client's membranes rupture spontaneously. The nurse notes the amniotic fluid is greenish-brown. What is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Assess the fetal heart rate (FHR) and pattern.
Perform a vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation.
Administer oxygen to the mother via non-rebreather mask.

questions.rationale: Greenish-brown amniotic fluid indicates the presence of meconium, which can be a sign of fetal distress (hypoxia) either past or present. Meconium aspiration syndrome is a potential complication for the newborn. The immediate priority is to assess fetal well-being by evaluating the FHR and pattern (C) for signs of distress (e.g., bradycardia, late decelerations, decreased variability). While notifying the provider (D) and potentially administering oxygen (A) are important, assessing the FHR takes precedence. A vaginal examination (B) is contraindicated when membranes are ruptured and meconium is present due to increased infection risk and potential to advance cord/meconium. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a postpartum client 24 hours after a vaginal delivery. The nurse palpates the uterine fundus. Where should the nurse expect to locate the fundus?

choices.text: Midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis
At the level of the symphysis pubis

questions.rationale: After delivery, the uterus begins the process of involution. By 24 hours postpartum, the fundus should typically be firm and located approximately 1 cm (or one fingerbreadth) below the umbilicus (B). It continues to descend about 1 cm per day, becoming non-palpable abdominally by around day 10-14. Locating it at the symphysis (A) occurs much later. Midway (C) would be around day 6. Above the umbilicus (D) immediately post-delivery is possible, but it should be below the umbilicus by 24 hours. This represents a key concept in maternity nursing.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, the thinning and shortening of the cervix during the first stage of labor is referred to as ____________.
questions.rationale: Effacement describes the process where the cervix, normally about 2-3 cm long, progressively thins out and shortens, drawing up into the lower uterine segment. It is measured in percentages (0% to 100%). Dilation refers to the opening of the cervical os. Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a client 4 hours after vaginal delivery. Which findings should the nurse report to the healthcare provider? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Urinary output of 100 mL in 4 hours

questions.rationale: Decreased urinary output, elevated temperature, and elevated blood pressure require immediate provider notification as they may indicate complications like infection or preeclampsia. Lochia rubra with small clots and a firm, midline fundus are normal findings in the immediate postpartum period. Understanding this concept is crucial for providing evidence-based care.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is caring for a woman in the first stage of labor. Which postpartum assessment finding indicates progression to the active phase?
questions.rationale: According to current labor guidelines, the active phase of labor begins at 6 cm dilation. 30% effacement (B) is insufficient for active labor. Station -3 (C) indicates the fetal head is still high in the pelvis. Contractions every 15 minutes (D) are too far apart for active labor, which typically has contractions every 2-3 minutes. Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a pregnant woman at 34 weeks gestation presents with sudden onset of severe right upper quadrant pain, headache, and visual disturbances. Her blood pressure is 168/110 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.rationale: The symptoms of severe right upper quadrant pain (liver involvement), headache, visual disturbances, and severely elevated blood pressure (≥160/110 mmHg) are diagnostic criteria for preeclampsia with severe features. Gestational diabetes (A) presents with glucose intolerance. Placenta previa (C) presents with painless vaginal bleeding. While HELLP syndrome (D) is a complication of preeclampsia, the symptoms described more directly point to preeclampsia with severe features. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a 1-hour-old newborn. Which postpartum assessment finding would require immediate intervention?

choices.text: Heart rate of 110 beats per minute
Respiratory rate of 68 breaths per minute

questions.rationale: Central cyanosis (blue discoloration of the trunk, lips, and mucous membranes) indicates significant hypoxemia and requires immediate intervention. Acrocyanosis (A) is a normal finding in newborns, characterized by bluish extremities with normal core coloration. A heart rate of 110 bpm (B) is within normal range (100-160 bpm). A respiratory rate of 68 breaths per minute (C) is slightly elevated but can be normal in the first hours of life (normal range 40-60). This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

Variable decelerations have a distinctive 'V' or 'U' shape on the monitor. Uteroplacental insufficiency (A) causes late decelerations, which have a gradual onset and recovery, occur after the peak of the contraction, and have a uniform shape. Maternal hypotension (C) typically causes prolonged decelerations. Fetal head compression (D) causes early decelerations, which mirror the contraction and rarely drop below 100 bpm. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a laboring client's membranes rupture spontaneously. the nurse notes the amniotic fluid is greenish-brown. what is the nurse's priority action?

choices.text: Perform a vaginal examination to assess cervical dilation.
Assess the fetal heart rate (FHR) and pattern.
Administer oxygen to the mother via non-rebreather mask.

questions.rationale: Greenish-brown amniotic fluid indicates the presence of meconium, which can be a sign of fetal distress (hypoxia) either past or present. Meconium aspiration syndrome is a potential complication for the newborn. The immediate priority is to assess fetal well-being by evaluating the FHR and pattern (C) for signs of distress (e.g., bradycardia, late decelerations, decreased variability). While notifying the provider (D) and potentially administering oxygen (A) are important, assessing the FHR takes precedence. A vaginal examination (B) is contraindicated when membranes are ruptured and meconium is present due to increased infection risk and potential to advance cord/meconium. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in maternity nursing.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is assessing a postpartum client 24 hours after a vaginal delivery. the nurse palpates the uterine fundus. where should the nurse expect to locate the fundus?

choices.text: At the level of the symphysis pubis
Midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis

questions.rationale: After delivery, the uterus begins the process of involution. By 24 hours postpartum, the fundus should typically be firm and located approximately 1 cm (or one fingerbreadth) below the umbilicus (B). It continues to descend about 1 cm per day, becoming non-palpable abdominally by around day 10-14. Locating it at the symphysis (A) occurs much later. Midway (C) would be around day 6. Above the umbilicus (D) immediately post-delivery is possible, but it should be below the umbilicus by 24 hours. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, the thinning and shortening of the cervix during the first stage of labor is referred to as ____________.
questions.rationale: Effacement describes the process where the cervix, normally about 2-3 cm long, progressively thins out and shortens, drawing up into the lower uterine segment. It is measured in percentages (0% to 100%). Dilation refers to the opening of the cervical os. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is assessing a client 4 hours after vaginal delivery. which findings should the nurse report to the healthcare provider? select all that apply.

choices.text: Urinary output of 100 mL in 4 hours

questions.rationale: Decreased urinary output, elevated temperature, and elevated blood pressure require immediate provider notification as they may indicate complications like infection or preeclampsia. Lochia rubra with small clots and a firm, midline fundus are normal findings in the immediate postpartum period. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is caring for a woman in the first stage of labor. which finding indicates progression to the active phase?
questions.rationale: According to current labor guidelines, the active phase of labor begins at 6 cm dilation. 30% effacement (B) is insufficient for active labor. Station -3 (C) indicates the fetal head is still high in the pelvis. Contractions every 15 minutes (D) are too far apart for active labor, which typically has contractions every 2-3 minutes. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Understanding this concept is crucial for providing evidence-based care.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a pregnant woman at 34 weeks gestation presents with sudden onset of severe right upper quadrant pain, headache, and visual disturbances. her blood pressure is 168/110 mmhg. what is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Gestational diabetes, as indicated by assessment findings

questions.rationale: The symptoms of severe right upper quadrant pain (liver involvement), headache, visual disturbances, and severely elevated blood pressure (≥160/110 mmHg) are diagnostic criteria for preeclampsia with severe features. Gestational diabetes (A) presents with glucose intolerance. Placenta previa (C) presents with painless vaginal bleeding. While HELLP syndrome (D) is a complication of preeclampsia, the symptoms described more directly point to preeclampsia with severe features. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Maternity Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is assessing a 1-hour-old newborn. which finding would require immediate intervention?

choices.text: Respiratory rate of 68 breaths per minute
Heart rate of 110 beats per minute

questions.rationale: Central cyanosis (blue discoloration of the trunk, lips, and mucous membranes) indicates significant hypoxemia and requires immediate intervention. Acrocyanosis (A) is a normal finding in newborns, characterized by bluish extremities with normal core coloration. A heart rate of 110 bpm (B) is within normal range (100-160 bpm). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in maternity nursing.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, in a hospital setting, a pregnant patient's last menstrual period (lmp) began on june 10th. using nägele's rule, what is the estimated date of delivery (edd)?
questions.rationale: Nägele's rule is calculated by subtracting 3 months from the first day of the LMP and adding 7 days. LMP: June 10th. Subtract 3 months: March 10th. Add 7 days: March 17th. Therefore, the EDD is March 17th. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Maternity
questions.text: Based on current best practices, in a hospital setting, a nurse is assessing a patient in the third trimester of pregnancy. which physiological change is considered normal during this period?

choices.text: Decrease in heart rate by 10 bpm.
Increase in respiratory rate and depth.

questions.rationale: During pregnancy, particularly the third trimester, progesterone causes increased sensitivity of the respiratory center to CO2 and maternal oxygen demands increase, leading to increased respiratory rate and tidal volume (depth) (B). Total blood volume significantly increases (A). Heart rate typically increases by 10-15 bpm (C). Dependent edema often worsens in the third trimester due to pressure from the enlarging uterus on pelvic veins and increased fluid volume (D). questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to Erikson's stages of psychosocial development, which developmental task is characteristic of the toddler age group (1-3 years)?
questions.rationale: Erikson's stage for toddlers (1-3 years) is Autonomy vs. Shame and Doubt (D). During this stage, toddlers focus on developing a sense of personal control over physical skills and a sense of independence. Trust vs. Mistrust (A) is infancy (birth-1 year). Initiative vs. Guilt (B) is preschool (3-6 years). Industry vs. Inferiority (C) is school-age (6-12 years). This information guides proper clinical decision-making.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a common childhood illness characterized by a high fever for several days followed by the appearance of a maculopapular rash after the fever breaks is known as Roseola infantum, caused by Human __________ 6 or 7.
questions.rationale: Roseola infantum (also called sixth disease or exanthem subitum) is typically caused by Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or less commonly HHV-7. The classic presentation involves 3-5 days of high fever which resolves abruptly, followed by the onset of a non-itchy, pinkish-red maculopapular rash that often starts on the trunk and spreads. Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. Which pediatric medication clinical sign would the nurse assess for that is characteristic of meningeal irritation?
questions.rationale: Meningeal irritation causes inflammation of the meninges, leading to specific signs. Brudzinski's sign is positive when passive flexion of the child's neck causes flexion of the hips and knees (C). Kernig's sign is positive when resistance or pain occurs upon extending the knee while the hip is flexed. Nuchal rigidity (stiff neck) is also common (B is incorrect). A negative Kernig's sign (A) indicates absence of that specific sign of irritation. A sunken fontanelle (D) suggests dehydration rather than meningitis, and by age 4, the fontanelles have typically closed. This reflects current best practices in nursing care.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected epiglottitis. Which intervention is the highest priority?

choices.text: Avoiding agitating the child and inspecting the throat
Placing the child in a mist tent

questions.rationale: In suspected epiglottitis, a potentially life-threatening condition, the highest priority is to avoid agitating the child and NOT to inspect the throat, as this could precipitate complete airway obstruction. A controlled environment should be maintained and an airway team should be present before any examination. While intubation (C) may be necessary, it should be done in a controlled setting. Antibiotics (A) and a mist tent (D) are important but secondary to airway management. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is administering immunizations to a 6-month-old infant. The parent asks why multiple vaccines are given at once. The nurse's best response is:

choices.text: 'It saves time and reduces the number of office visits needed.'
'All of the above are accurate explanations.'
'The immune system can respond to millions of antigens at once, so multiple vaccines are safe.'
'If we delay vaccines, your child could contract serious diseases during the waiting period.'

questions.rationale: All three statements are accurate explanations for administering multiple vaccines at once. The immune system can effectively respond to multiple antigens simultaneously (B). Combining vaccines reduces the number of visits needed (A), and delaying vaccines leaves children vulnerable to preventable diseases (C). Providing comprehensive education helps address parental concerns about vaccination schedules. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a 3-year-old child is admitted with suspected intussusception. Which assessment finding is most characteristic of this condition?
questions.rationale: Currant jelly-like stools (dark red, mucoid stools) are the classic sign of intussusception due to intestinal bleeding and mucus production where one segment of bowel telescopes into another. Projectile vomiting (A) is more characteristic of pyloric stenosis. Clay-colored stools (C) suggest biliary obstruction. Severe diarrhea with mucus (D) is more typical of infectious gastroenteritis. Understanding this concept is crucial for providing evidence-based care.

questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected epiglottitis. which intervention is the highest priority?

choices.text: Avoiding agitating the child and inspecting the throat
Placing the child in a mist tent

questions.rationale: In suspected epiglottitis, a potentially life-threatening condition, the highest priority is to avoid agitating the child and NOT to inspect the throat, as this could precipitate complete airway obstruction. A controlled environment should be maintained and an airway team should be present before any examination. While intubation (C) may be necessary, it should be done in a controlled setting. Antibiotics (A) and a mist tent (D) are important but secondary to airway management. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in pediatric nursing.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a nurse is administering immunizations to a 6-month-old infant. the parent asks why multiple vaccines are given at once. the nurse's best response is:

choices.text: 'If we delay vaccines, your child could contract serious diseases during the waiting period.'
'It saves time and reduces the number of office visits needed.'
'All of the above are accurate explanations.'
'The immune system can respond to millions of antigens at once, so multiple vaccines are safe.'

questions.rationale: All three statements are accurate explanations for administering multiple vaccines at once. The immune system can effectively respond to multiple antigens simultaneously (B). Combining vaccines reduces the number of visits needed (A), and delaying vaccines leaves children vulnerable to preventable diseases (C). Providing comprehensive education helps address parental concerns about vaccination schedules. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, according to nursing standards, a 3-year-old child is admitted with suspected intussusception. which assessment finding is most characteristic of this condition?

choices.text: Currant jelly-like stools, following best practice guidelines
Severe diarrhea with mucus, as indicated by assessment findings
Projectile vomiting after feeding, following best practice guidelines

questions.rationale: Currant jelly-like stools (dark red, mucoid stools) are the classic sign of intussusception due to intestinal bleeding and mucus production where one segment of bowel telescopes into another. Projectile vomiting (A) is more characteristic of pyloric stenosis. Clay-colored stools (C) suggest biliary obstruction. Severe diarrhea with mucus (D) is more typical of infectious gastroenteritis. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Application of this principle promotes positive patient outcomes.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, in a hospital setting, according to erikson's stages of psychosocial development, which developmental task is characteristic of the toddler age group (1-3 years)?

choices.text: Autonomy vs. Shame and Doubt, as indicated by assessment findings

questions.rationale: Erikson's stage for toddlers (1-3 years) is Autonomy vs. Shame and Doubt (D). During this stage, toddlers focus on developing a sense of personal control over physical skills and a sense of independence. Trust vs. Mistrust (A) is infancy (birth-1 year). Initiative vs. Guilt (B) is preschool (3-6 years). Industry vs. Inferiority (C) is school-age (6-12 years). This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, in a hospital setting, a common childhood illness characterized by a high fever for several days followed by the appearance of a maculopapular rash after the fever breaks is known as roseola infantum, caused by human __________ 6 or 7.
questions.rationale: Roseola infantum (also called sixth disease or exanthem subitum) is typically caused by Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6) or less commonly HHV-7. The classic presentation involves 3-5 days of high fever which resolves abruptly, followed by the onset of a non-itchy, pinkish-red maculopapular rash that often starts on the trunk and spreads. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This represents a key concept in pediatric nursing.

questions.title: Pediatric Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, in a hospital setting, a 4-year-old child is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. which clinical sign would the nurse assess for that is characteristic of meningeal irritation?
questions.rationale: Meningeal irritation causes inflammation of the meninges, leading to specific signs. Brudzinski's sign is positive when passive flexion of the child's neck causes flexion of the hips and knees (C). Kernig's sign is positive when resistance or pain occurs upon extending the knee while the hip is flexed. Nuchal rigidity (stiff neck) is also common (B is incorrect). A negative Kernig's sign (A) indicates absence of that specific sign of irritation. A sunken fontanelle (D) suggests dehydration rather than meningitis, and by age 4, the fontanelles have typically closed. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client diagnosed with major depressive disorder states, "Nothing matters anymore. It's hopeless." Which crisis management response by the nurse is most therapeutic?

choices.text: "Let's focus on something positive, like your hobbies.", as indicated by assessment findings
"Why do you think everything is hopeless?"
"It sounds like you're feeling very discouraged right now."
"You shouldn't feel that way; things will get better.", which is recommended for this situation

questions.rationale: Reflecting the client's feelings ('feeling very discouraged') validates their experience and opens the door for further exploration (C). Offering false reassurance (A) dismisses the client's feelings. Asking 'why' questions (B) can sound accusatory and put the client on the defensive. Changing the subject abruptly (D) avoids the client's expressed concern. Understanding this concept is crucial for providing evidence-based care.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client taking haloperidol (Haldol) develops uncontrollable restlessness, pacing, and fidgeting. The nurse recognizes these symptoms as indicative of which extrapyramidal side effect (EPS)?
questions.rationale: Akathisia (B) is characterized by motor restlessness, with an inability to sit still, constant pacing, fidgeting, and agitation. This differs from tardive dyskinesia (A), which involves involuntary, repetitive movements, particularly of the face and mouth. Acute dystonia (C) presents as sudden muscle spasms and abnormal posturing. Pseudoparkinsonism (D) involves tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and shuffling gait. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a client with anorexia nervosa has a nursing diagnosis of Imbalanced Nutrition: Less Than Body Requirements. Which intervention is most important for the nurse to implement initially?

choices.text: Explore the client's feelings about weight gain.
Provide education on the long-term health consequences of malnutrition.
Encourage the client to lead group therapy sessions.
Establish a structured meal plan with specific times and amounts.

questions.rationale: For clients with anorexia nervosa, establishing nutritional stability is a priority. Implementing a structured meal plan with clear expectations for intake (B) provides routine and minimizes negotiation or avoidance behaviors around food. While exploring feelings (A) and providing education (C) are important therapeutic interventions, addressing the immediate nutritional deficit through structured eating is often the initial focus. Leading group therapy (D) is not appropriate initially and would not address the nutritional deficit. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient is experiencing significant anxiety related to a recent diagnosis. Which nursing intervention demonstrates a holistic approach to care?

choices.text: Facilitating communication with family and support systems.

questions.rationale: A holistic approach involves addressing the patient's physical, emotional, and social needs. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient diagnosed with major depressive disorder has been prescribed sertraline (Zoloft). The nurse should inform the patient that:

choices.text: The full therapeutic effect may take 2-4 weeks
The medication should be discontinued once symptoms improve
The medication will start working immediately to improve mood
Alcohol consumption will enhance the medication's effectiveness

questions.rationale: SSRIs like sertraline (Zoloft) typically take 2-4 weeks to reach full therapeutic effect, although some side effects and initial changes may occur sooner. It's important for patients to continue taking the medication even if they don't feel immediate improvement. SSRIs do not work immediately (A), should not be discontinued when symptoms improve without provider guidance (C), and alcohol should be avoided as it can worsen depression and interact with the medication (D). This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a nurse is assessing a patient who states, 'The CIA has implanted a chip in my brain to monitor my thoughts.' This statement most likely indicates:
questions.rationale: This statement represents a delusion, which is a fixed, false belief that cannot be corrected by reasoning and is not consistent with the person's cultural background. Specifically, this is a persecutory delusion involving beliefs about being monitored or targeted. An illusion (A) is a misperception of a real external stimulus. A hallucination (C) involves perception without external stimuli. Flight of ideas (D) refers to rapid shifting between topics in conversation. This represents a key concept in mental health nursing.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, a patient with bipolar disorder is prescribed lithium carbonate. Which laboratory value is most important for the nurse to monitor?
questions.rationale: Serum lithium levels must be closely monitored due to lithium's narrow therapeutic index. Toxic levels can develop quickly and cause serious adverse effects including tremors, ataxia, confusion, seizures, coma, and death. The therapeutic range is 0.6-1.2 mEq/L, with toxicity often occurring above 1.5 mEq/L. Hemoglobin A1C (A), total cholesterol (C), and prothrombin time (D) are not directly relevant to lithium therapy. This represents a key concept in mental health nursing.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a client diagnosed with major depressive disorder states, "nothing matters anymore. it's hopeless." which response by the nurse is most therapeutic?

choices.text: "You shouldn't feel that way; things will get better."
"Let's focus on something positive, like your hobbies."
"Why do you think everything is hopeless?"
"It sounds like you're feeling very discouraged right now."

questions.rationale: Reflecting the client's feelings ('feeling very discouraged') validates their experience and opens the door for further exploration (C). Offering false reassurance (A) dismisses the client's feelings. Asking 'why' questions (B) can sound accusatory and put the client on the defensive. Changing the subject abruptly (D) avoids the client's expressed concern. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. This knowledge helps nurses prioritize interventions appropriately.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a client taking haloperidol (haldol) develops uncontrollable restlessness, pacing, and fidgeting. the nurse recognizes these symptoms as indicative of which extrapyramidal side effect (eps)?
questions.rationale: Akathisia (B) is characterized by motor restlessness, with an inability to sit still, constant pacing, fidgeting, and agitation. This differs from tardive dyskinesia (A), which involves involuntary, repetitive movements, particularly of the face and mouth. Acute dystonia (C) presents as sudden muscle spasms and abnormal posturing. Pseudoparkinsonism (D) involves tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and shuffling gait. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

questions.title: Mental Health Nursing
questions.text: Based on current best practices, based on current best practices, a client with anorexia nervosa has a nursing diagnosis of imbalanced nutrition: less than body requirements. which intervention is most important for the nurse to implement initially?

choices.text: Explore the client's feelings about weight gain.
Encourage the client to lead group therapy sessions., as indicated by assessment findings
Establish a structured meal plan with specific times and amounts.
Provide education on the long-term health consequences of malnutrition.

questions.rationale: For clients with anorexia nervosa, establishing nutritional stability is a priority. Implementing a structured meal plan with clear expectations for intake (B) provides routine and minimizes negotiation or avoidance behaviors around food. While exploring feelings (A) and providing education (C) are important therapeutic interventions, addressing the immediate nutritional deficit through structured eating is often the initial focus. Leading group therapy (D) is not appropriate initially and would not address the nutritional deficit. This principle is important for safe, effective nursing practice. Recognizing this is essential for maintaining patient safety.

The medication will start working immediately to improve mood
